html:
HOW TO

1. Watch the response time trend.

Three days became five. Five became a week. Each follow-up takes longer. By the time the developer hasn't responded in two weeks, they're already gone — they just haven't said it. Track response times. When they double, raise the issue directly.

2. Note whether they've moved you to a calendar.

Healthy projects have scheduled check-ins. Ghost-bound projects have only ad-hoc messages, and those get sparser. If the developer can't commit to a weekly 20-minute call, they're already over-extended.

3. Ask for access to the source code, hosting, and domain — now.

If you don't have logins to your own GitHub repo, Netlify or Vercel account, domain registrar, and database, you don't own your website. Request access in writing. A real developer adds you immediately. A ghosting developer makes excuses.

4. Check whether they've delegated.

Is the work suddenly being done by someone else with no warning? Is the email coming from a different name? Subcontracting itself isn't a problem — silent subcontracting is.

5. Look at their other work.

Their other client sites: still online and updated, or stale? Their portfolio: refreshed in the last six months, or frozen? Their social: posting, or silent? Developers who are about to ghost have usually already started receding from their other commitments.

6. Get a writeable inventory of everything they've built.

What lives where. What credentials exist. What contracts cover what. If they won't write this down, that's the answer. If they will, you have what you need to bring in someone else when the time comes.

Most ghosting isn't malicious — it's overcommitment. The developer took on too much work, can't deliver, and is too embarrassed to say so. Either way, you're left holding the bag. Little Fight NYC takes over from ghosted developers regularly. The consult is free, and if you call early enough we can usually save the project. Call (646) 360-0318.
